cot:
Okay, so I'm trying to figure out whether to take just container Q, or both containers Q and R in this scenario. Let me break it down step by step.

First, I need to understand the setup. There are two containers, R and Q. Container R always has 60 points, no matter what. Container Q is a bit trickier. If F, the system, predicted that I would take only Q, then Q has 100 points. But if F predicted that I would take both Q and R, then Q has 0 points. Also, F correctly identifies my choices 80% of the time, which is pretty accurate. I can't see what's in the containers before deciding, and whatever I take, I get to keep.

Hmm, so the key here is understanding F's predictions and how that affects the points in Q. Since F is predicting my choice, if F thinks I'll take only Q, then Q is 100 points. If it thinks I'll take both, Q is 0. So, if I take both, Q could be either 100 or 0, depending on F's prediction.

Wait, no. Let me clarify. It says F holds 60 points in R regardless. So R is fixed at 60. Q's points depend on F's prediction of my choice. If F thinks I'll take only Q, Q is 100. If it thinks I'll take both, Q is 0. So, if I take both, then Q is 0 because F predicted that. If I take only Q, then Q is 100.

But since F is predicting my choice, and it's 80% accurate, that means there's an 80% chance F is correct and a 20% chance it's wrong. So, if I decide to take both, F might be correct 80% of the time, making Q 0. If I decide to take only Q, F might be correct 80% of the time, giving me 100 points, but 20% of the time F is wrong, meaning Q would have 100 points 20% of the time and 0 the other 80%.

Wait, no. Let me think again. If I choose to take both, F's prediction is that I'll take both, so Q becomes 0. So, if I choose L (take both), I get R (60) and Q (0). If I choose J (take only Q), then
